Title: Natural products of pentacyclic triterpenoids: from discovery to heterologous biosynthesis

Common_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Not specified; review mentions promoter modification generally (e.g., GAL1, PGK1, UPC2-binding synthetic promoters) in heterologous systems
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: HMGR is a core upstream control point for boosting pentacyclic triterpenoid precursor supply. Suitable for overexpression, promoter strengthening, elicitor-responsive induction, and stacking with FPS/SS/SE/OSC modules in plant or microbial chassis. Use with caution because excessive flux can perturb sterol/isoprenoid balance.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Increased HMGR expression generally promotes precursor supply and accumulation of pentacyclic triterpenoids such as oleanolic acid and related saponins.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Elicitor treatment-expression correlation; overexpression in heterologous or native systems; metabolite profiling
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: MeJA; SA; auxin; GA; light; temperature; humidity; salt; soil factors
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Induced under elicitor/stress conditions associated with triterpenoid accumulation
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: Review refers generally to metabolomics, transcriptomics, genomics, multiomics; no unified accession for this review
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Described as the first important rate-limiting enzyme and regulatory site in the MVA pathway.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Flux competition with sterol/isoprenoid metabolism; possible metabolic burden or toxic intermediate imbalance in heterologous systems
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. HMGR is a major upstream rate-limiting step in the mevalonate pathway supporting pentacyclic triterpenoid biosynthesis. 2. Its transcript abundance commonly rises after elicitor treatment and correlates with increased triterpenoid accumulation. 3. Engineering HMGR is a standard strategy to increase precursor flux in plant and microbial platforms.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Various plant tissues; also engineered microbial/plant hosts
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, hepatoprotective, antiviral, anticancer and other bioactivities

Common_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Not specified; review discusses promoter engineering in heterologous hosts
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: FPS is useful to increase FPP supply and redirect carbon toward squalene/triterpenoid production. Best deployed with HMGR/SS/SE and downstream OSC-CYP-UGT modules.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Enhanced FPS expression promotes formation of triterpenoid precursors and can raise betulinic acid, oleanolic acid, betulin, and related metabolite levels.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene expression-metabolite correlation; overexpression studies; precursor/metabolite quantification
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: General elicitor and environmental regulation discussed; not consistently gene-specific
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Induced in conditions favoring triterpenoid synthesis
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: General multiomics mentioned; no specific accession
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: FPS regulates synthesis of FPP, a central branch precursor for terpenoids, and influences carbon-flow direction.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Competes with other FPP-consuming pathways; excessive rerouting may affect growth or sterol homeostasis
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. FPS supplies FPP for squalene formation and downstream triterpenoid biosynthesis. 2. Increasing FPS expression can elevate precursor pools and final pentacyclic triterpenoids. 3. It is a common engineering target for pathway flux enhancement.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Bioactive natural products with medicinal and stress-related functions

Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: SS is a key engineering node linking FPP to squalene. Overexpression or elicitor induction can improve triterpenoid biosynthesis, especially when paired with SE and downstream modules.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Higher SS expression generally enhances squalene formation and supports increased pentacyclic triterpenoid accumulation.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Elicitor-induced expression studies; overexpression; metabolite analyses
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: MeJA; SA; GA; cold stress; shading percentage
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Induced by elicitor and environmental factors associated with triterpenoid accumulation
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: General multiomics mentioned; no specific accession
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: SS catalyzes the key conversion of FPP to squalene and is important in both triterpene saponin and phytosterol biosynthesis.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: May affect sterol metabolism and membrane homeostasis if flux is strongly redirected
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SS converts FPP into squalene, a committed precursor for triterpenoid biosynthesis. 2. MeJA and other factors can induce SS expression. 3. SS is a strong leverage point for increasing triterpenoid precursor supply in engineering systems.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Medicinal and ecological bioactivities attributed to pentacyclic triterpenoids

Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: SE is a core precursor-forming step and should be co-engineered with HMGR/FPS/SS and downstream OSCs. Valuable in both plant and microbial chassis to raise 2,3-oxidosqualene supply.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Increased SE activity/expression enhances 2,3-oxidosqualene formation and promotes downstream pentacyclic triterpenoid biosynthesis.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Elicitor response assays; overexpression in pathway engineering; metabolite profiling
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: General multiomics mentioned; no specific accession
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: SE catalyzes epoxidation of squalene to 2,3-oxidosqualene, the direct substrate for OSCs.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Precursor reallocation may influence sterol pathway and cellular fitness in engineered hosts
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SE produces 2,3-oxidosqualene, the immediate substrate for triterpene cyclization. 2. Its expression is inducible by elicitors such as SA and MeJA. 3. SE is a standard engineering target for improving pentacyclic triterpenoid biosynthesis.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: OSC is the key entry enzyme to diversify triterpene skeletons. Select specific OSCs for desired scaffold output (β-amyrin, α-amyrin, lupeol, friedelin, etc.) and stack with tailored CYPs and UGTs in yeast, Nicotiana, or other chassis.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Cyclizes 2,3-oxidosqualene into diverse tetracyclic or pentacyclic triterpene skeletons through protonation, cyclization, rearrangement, and deprotonation cascades.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Determines triterpene skeleton identity and branch selection, thereby controlling which pentacyclic triterpenoid class can accumulate.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Heterologous expression in yeast/plants; product profiling by GC/LC-MS; phylogenetic and functional characterization
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms core scaffold/branch point precursor for all downstream pentacyclic triterpenoids
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Not a major environmental focus for this enzyme family in the review
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Varies by species and triterpenoid-producing tissue; not systematically summarized in the review
Pathway_Genes.Gene_Accession_Number: Family-level review; multiple IDs summarized in Table 1
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: General genomics/transcriptomics/metabolomics used for gene mining; no single accession
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: A single amino-acid change can alter product spectrum. Over 100 triterpene skeletons arose via OSC divergence. Includes unusual rice OsOSC7 generating orysatinol/orisaspirol-like products through chair-half chair-chair conformation.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Multifunctional OSCs can generate mixed products, reducing product purity in engineering systems
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: β-Amyrin; α-amyrin; lupeol; friedelin; germanicol; taraxasterol; taraxerol; others
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: First rate-limiting enzyme in downstream triterpenoid saponin synthesis; key branch-control step
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. OSCs create structural diversity by converting 2,3-oxidosqualene into distinct triterpene skeletons. 2. Functional classes cluster phylogenetically according to product type. 3. OSC choice is foundational for heterologous production of specific pentacyclic triterpenoids.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Medicinal bioactivities and ecological defense functions

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: CYPs should be selected according to desired oxidation pattern (C-28, C-23, C-24, C-2α, C-6β, C-11, C-16, epoxidation, etc.). Excellent targets for scaffold decoration and scaffold-specific product diversification in synthetic biology.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxidizes triterpene skeletons via hydroxylation, carboxylation, epoxidation, ketone/aldehyde formation, dehydrogenation, and related reactions at specific carbon positions.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Introduces key oxidative decorations that convert simple triterpene scaffolds into bioactive intermediates and end products such as oleanolic acid, ursolic acid, betulinic acid, maslinic acid, corosolic acid, glycyrrhetinic acid, and soyasapogenols.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Yeast or plant heterologous expression; co-expression with OSC and CPR; substrate feeding; product profiling
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Converts core scaffold into immediate intermediates or direct end products; branch-specific decoration steps
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Not a major direct environmental focus at family level
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Varies across species and specialized metabolite tissues
Pathway_Genes.Gene_Accession_Number: Family-level review; multiple IDs in Table 2
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: CPR (cytochrome P450 reductase) commonly required in heterologous systems
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Midstream to downstream decoration steps after scaffold cyclization
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Major families include CYP51, CYP71, CYP72, CYP85; CYP716 family dominates dicot pentacyclic triterpene oxidation; monocot-specific CYP51H subfamily is notable in oats.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Low heterologous activity, poor regio/chemospecificity, membrane-association constraints, by-product formation
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Oxidized triterpenes such as oleanolic acid, ursolic acid, betulinic acid, hederagenin, maslinic acid, corosolic acid, glycyrrhetinic acid, soyasapogenols
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: β-Amyrin; α-amyrin; lupeol; oleanolic acid; ursolic acid; and related intermediates
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control/decorative steps; often critical bottlenecks in pathway reconstruction
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CYP450s are the principal oxidative tailoring enzymes generating pentacyclic triterpenoid structural diversity. 2. Family membership often correlates with reaction position and product type. 3. CYP choice strongly determines end-product identity and is a major bottleneck/opportunity in heterologous biosynthesis.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: UGTs are final-step engineering targets to create saponins with improved solubility, storage, and bioactivity. Should be paired with appropriate oxidized aglycone modules and donor-sugar supply engineering.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers sugar moieties from activated donors such as UDP-glucose, UDP-glucuronic acid, UDP-rhamnose, or UDP-xylose to triterpene aglycones or partially glycosylated intermediates.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Forms diverse triterpenoid saponins and determines glycosylation pattern, a major contributor to compound diversity and bioactivity.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: In vitro enzyme assays; heterologous expression in E. coli/yeast; metabolite profiling; gene co-expression analyses
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Not a major direct environmental focus at family level
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Varies across species and metabolite-accumulating tissues
Pathway_Genes.Gene_Accession_Number: Family-level review; multiple IDs in Table 3
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: General transcriptome-metabolome co-analysis used for candidate discovery; no single accession
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: UGT73 family is especially prominent; regioselectivity can target C-3, C-22, C-28, C-29/C-30 and others. The review also notes GT2 cellulose-synthase-derived enzymes and GH1 AsTG1 with UGT-like activity.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Strong substrate promiscuity and donor complexity complicate prediction and engineering; donor sugar limitation in microbes is a major bottleneck
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Triterpenoid saponins such as glycyrrhizin and glycosylated derivatives of oleanolic acid, hederagenin, soyasapogenols, etc.
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: Oxidized triterpene aglycones and partially glycosylated intermediates
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Terminal determinant of saponin diversity and bioactivity; key but not universally rate-limiting
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Not clearly summarized; generally cytosolic glycosyltransferase context
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. UGT-catalyzed glycosylation is usually the last major step in pentacyclic triterpenoid saponin formation. 2. It is crucial for chemical diversity, solubility, storage, and bioactivity. 3. UGT engineering requires matching donor supply, acceptor specificity, and regioselectivity.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Medicinal, defensive, and physicochemical functions linked to glycosylation

Regulation_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Bind promoters of key pathway genes such as HMGR, FPS, SS, SE and other triterpenoid biosynthetic genes; specific promoter names not systematically listed in this review
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: AP2/ERF factors are attractive master regulators for coordinated activation of multiple triterpenoid pathway genes and elicitor-responsive production systems in plants or engineered hosts.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Can activate or inhibit key biosynthetic genes, altering pentacyclic triterpenoid accumulation under elicitor/stress conditions.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transcriptional studies, promoter-binding/transactivation evidence summarized from cited literature; metabolite correlation
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Recognized as one of the major TF families controlling triterpenoid metabolism and elicitor responsiveness.
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: MeJA and other elicitors/stresses often act upstream
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Inducible under stress/elicitor conditions linked to specialized metabolism
Regulation_Genes.Omics_Data: General transcriptomics/multiomics used to mine regulators
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Review treats this family at regulator-class level rather than detailing individual genes.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Pleiotropic effects on stress signaling and growth may occur when overexpressed
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: HMGR; FPS; SS; SE; possibly downstream triterpenoid biosynthetic genes
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Activates or inhibits key rate-limiting enzyme genes depending on context
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AP2/ERF TFs are prominent regulators of plant triterpenoid metabolism. 2. They connect elicitor signaling to pathway gene expression. 3. They are promising system-level engineering targets for coordinated metabolite enhancement.
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: MeJA; SA; ABA; GA; auxin; environmental stresses

Regulation_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Key biosynthetic gene promoters including HMGR/FPS/SS/SE and related pathway genes
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: bHLH TFs are useful for coordinated enhancement of upstream and downstream triterpenoid biosynthesis, especially in MeJA-responsive contexts. Candidate regulators for pathway-wide engineering in plant chassis.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Modulate accumulation of pentacyclic triterpenoids by regulating expression of core biosynthetic genes.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Literature summarized for transcriptional regulation, inducible expression, and metabolite association
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: A major reported TF family in terpenoid metabolism, often linked to elicitor-induced pathway activation.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Review cites bHLH as one of the principal TF families for terpenoid regulation.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Possible broader developmental or stress-related pleiotropy
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: HMGR; FPS; SS; SE and related biosynthetic genes
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Activates or modulates key pathway genes
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. bHLH TFs are recurrent positive regulators in triterpenoid metabolism studies. 2. They often mediate MeJA-linked induction of pathway genes. 3. They are suitable engineering nodes for coordinated control of precursor and product synthesis.
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Specialized metabolite functions including defense and pharmacological activity

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: WRKY TFs can be used to couple defense signaling with enhanced triterpenoid production and may suit inducible engineering strategies.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Regulate pentacyclic triterpenoid biosynthesis by altering transcription of pathway genes under elicitor or stress conditions.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Summarized regulatory studies from cited literature
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Included among the main TF families reported to regulate plant triterpenoid metabolism.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Discussed as a major regulatory family rather than as specific named genes in this review.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Potential cross-talk with immunity and defense networks
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: Key rate-limiting enzyme genes and other biosynthetic genes
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. WRKY TFs contribute to transcriptional control of triterpenoid biosynthesis. 2. They often connect defense/elicitor cues to specialized metabolism. 3. They are relevant engineering targets for inducible metabolite enhancement.

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: bZIP factors may coordinate hormonal/environmental regulation of triterpenoid metabolism and are potential upstream engineering nodes.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Influence pentacyclic triterpenoid accumulation through transcriptional regulation of biosynthetic genes.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Recognized as one of the main regulator families involved in terpenoid metabolism.
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Hormones and environmental factors discussed broadly
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Only family-level mention in this review.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Possible wider impacts on hormone signaling and stress responses
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. bZIP TFs are part of the known transcriptional control network of plant triterpenoid biosynthesis. 2. They likely relay hormonal/environmental information to pathway genes. 3. They are candidate regulators for systems-level engineering.

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: MYB TFs are useful for transcriptional rewiring of triterpenoid pathways and may be combined with bHLH/AP2-ERF regulators for stronger activation.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Regulate key triterpenoid biosynthetic genes and affect metabolite accumulation.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Literature synthesis from cited studies; pathway gene expression and metabolite correlation
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Listed among principal TF families regulating plant triterpenoid metabolism.
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: MeJA and other environmental/hormonal signals broadly implicated
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Review cites MYB involvement, including MeJA-induced regulation in birch triterpene pathway context.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: May alter broad secondary metabolism and developmental traits
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: HMGR; FPS; SS; SE and related genes
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Regulates key biosynthetic genes in triterpenoid pathways
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MYB TFs participate in controlling pentacyclic triterpenoid biosynthesis. 2. They can respond to elicitor signaling and regulate multiple pathway genes. 3. They are promising components for pathway rewiring in plant engineering.

Regulation_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Targets key rate-limiting enzyme genes or transcription factors at post-transcriptional level; not promoter-based
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: miRNAs offer fine-tuning tools for post-transcriptional control of triterpenoid pathways, useful for balancing flux and reducing pleiotropic cost compared with constitutive TF overexpression.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Post-transcriptionally regulates pentacyclic triterpenoid biosynthesis by repressing pathway enzymes or TFs through cleavage/splicing/translation control.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Functional prediction and limited reports summarized in review; high-throughput sequencing-based miRNA discovery
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Proposed as an important emerging regulatory layer, though mechanistic evidence remains limited.
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Stress and signaling pathways may influence miRNA action; details sparse
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: Potential homeostatic post-transcriptional tuning; not established
Regulation_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: Targets pathway genes or TF transcripts
Regulation_Genes.Omics_Data: High-throughput sequencing and bioinformatic prediction referenced generally
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The review emphasizes that this field is still in its infancy and many miRNA–target relationships remain predictive.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Off-target regulation and broad network effects possible
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: HMGR; FPS; SS; SE; triterpenoid-related TFs
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: RISC/miRNA silencing machinery implied but not detailed
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: mRNA cleavage; splicing/translation control; post-transcriptional silencing
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. miRNAs are an emerging regulatory layer in pentacyclic triterpenoid biosynthesis. 2. They likely act by targeting key enzymes or transcription factors. 3. Current evidence is limited, but they represent promising tools for precise pathway tuning.
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Developmental and stress/hormonal cues; specifics not resolved
